ideas.llmHelp: Set LLM_PROVIDER + credentials in .env to enable AI-powered trade ideas
ideas.disclosure: FOR INFORMATIONAL PURPOSES ONLY. This is not financial advice, a recommendation to buy or sell any security, or a solicitation of any kind. All signal-based observations are derived from publicly available OSINT data and should not be relied upon for investment decisions. Consult a licensed financial advisor before making any investment. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

commands.status: System health, last sweep time, source status

llm.systemPrompt:
You are a quantitative analyst at a macro intelligence firm. You receive structured OSINT + economic data from 25 sources and produce 5-8 actionable trade ideas.

Rules:
- Each idea must cite specific data points from the input
- Include entry rationale, risk factors, and time horizon
- Blend geopolitical, economic, and market signals — cross-correlate across domains
- Be specific: name instruments (tickers, futures, ETFs), not vague sectors
- If delta shows significant changes, lead with those
- Do NOT repeat ideas from the "previous ideas" list unless conditions have materially changed
- Rate confidence: HIGH (multiple confirming signals), MEDIUM (thesis supported), LOW (speculative)

Output ONLY valid JSON array. Each object:
{
  "title": "Short title (max 10 words)",
  "type": "LONG|SHORT|HEDGE|WATCH|AVOID",
  "ticker": "Primary instrument",
  "confidence": "HIGH|MEDIUM|LOW",
  "rationale": "2-3 sentence explanation citing specific data",
  "risk": "Key risk factor",
  "horizon": "Intraday|Days|Weeks|Months",
  "signals": ["signal1", "signal2"]
}

errors.noDataYet: No data yet — first sweep in progress

glossary.title: What the signals actually mean
glossary.subtitle: Plain-English interpretation, why it matters, and what you should not infer from it.
glossary.foot: Treat these as interpretation guides, not conclusions. Stronger judgments should come from corroboration across multiple layers, not from a single signal viewed in isolation.

items.meaning: OpenSky received an aircraft track without a usable callsign or flight ID in that record.
items.matters: Useful as an opacity signal. A cluster of missing callsigns can indicate incomplete transponder metadata or less transparent traffic.
items.notMeaning: Not proof of military, covert, or hostile activity on its own.
items.example: South China Sea: No Callsign 6 of 152 means 6 tracks in that theater had no usable callsign in the feed.

items.meaning: Aircraft above 12,000 meters, roughly 39,000 feet, in the current OpenSky snapshot.
items.matters: Separates cruise-level traffic from lower-altitude local or regional movement.
items.notMeaning: Not a danger score and not inherently unusual. Commercial jets commonly operate here.
items.example: High Altitude 2 means only 2 tracked aircraft in that hotspot were above the cruise threshold at that snapshot.

items.meaning: The most common OpenSky origin_country values among aircraft in that hotspot.
items.matters: Useful for understanding the rough composition of traffic flowing through a theater.
items.notMeaning: Not who is controlling the aircraft right now and not a direct indicator of military ownership.
items.example: China (61), Philippines (39), Taiwan (17) means those were the top registered origin countries in the snapshot.

items.meaning: Fire Radiative Power. This is the intensity of one specific FIRMS hotspot, measured in megawatts.
items.matters: Higher FRP usually means a hotter, larger, or more energetic fire event at that exact point.
items.notMeaning: Not the intensity of the whole region and not automatic proof of conflict activity.
items.example: Sudan / Horn of Africa: FRP 92.3 MW describes that one hotspot, while Total 1,451 describes the entire regional detection count.

items.meaning: The total number of FIRMS thermal detections in the entire region bucket for the current sweep.
items.matters: Useful for spotting unusually active fire clusters, especially when compared with historical baselines or night activity.
items.notMeaning: Not a count for the single map point you clicked and not necessarily a conflict count.
items.example: Total 1,451 means the whole Sudan / Horn of Africa bucket had 1,451 detections in that sweep.

items.meaning: Thermal detections tagged as occurring at night inside the broader FIRMS region bucket.
items.matters: Nighttime heat can be more noteworthy because it is less likely to be routine daytime land burning.
items.notMeaning: Not a direct combat indicator. It still needs context from location, baseline, and corroborating sources.
items.example: Night 140 means 140 of the 1,451 regional detections were nighttime detections in that sweep.

items.meaning: A strategic maritime corridor or passage where trade and energy flows can be delayed, diverted, or disrupted.
items.matters: These nodes matter because a disruption here can affect shipping costs, transit times, and commodity pricing globally.
items.notMeaning: Not proof that disruption is happening now. It is a strategic watch location.
items.example: Bab el-Mandeb or the Strait of Hormuz matter because shipping and energy flows concentrate there.

items.meaning: A publicly reachable software-defined radio receiver in or near a region of interest.
items.matters: Dense receiver coverage can give you more ability to monitor communications or signal activity in a theater.
items.notMeaning: Not evidence of hostile emissions or a threat by itself. It is an observation and monitoring layer.
items.example: South China Sea SDR count means publicly accessible KiwiSDR receivers are available in or near that zone.

items.meaning: Counts per minute from a radiation monitoring source, used here for relative radiation status at a site.
items.matters: Useful for spotting anomalies against the site's normal range or comparing consecutive readings.
items.notMeaning: Not a direct safety verdict on its own. Interpretation depends on local baseline and trend, not the raw number alone.
items.example: A site reading 33 CPM can be normal if that location's usual background level is in the same range.

items.meaning: High-yield credit spread, shown here as a stress proxy from FRED credit data.
items.matters: When spreads widen, markets are usually pricing more credit stress and tighter financial conditions.
items.notMeaning: Not a recession call by itself. It is one stress signal among many.
items.example: A rising HY Spread alongside higher VIX and weaker equities is a stronger risk-off pattern than HY alone.

items.meaning: The CBOE Volatility Index, commonly used as a market-implied fear or volatility gauge.
items.matters: Higher VIX often means more expected equity volatility and more defensive market positioning.
items.notMeaning: Not a direct forecast of a crash and not a geopolitical indicator by itself.
items.example: VIX above 20 with widening HY spreads is a stronger stress pattern than VIX alone.

items.meaning: The Global Supply Chain Pressure Index, a broad indicator of global supply-chain strain.
items.matters: It helps translate geopolitical or weather disruptions into likely pressure on shipping, inventory, and pricing.
items.notMeaning: Not a live market price and not a company-specific supply-chain score by itself.
items.example: A higher GSCPI makes route or energy shocks more likely to spill into broader cost pressure.

items.meaning: A WHO Disease Outbreak News item or outbreak-related bulletin surfaced in the health layer.
items.matters: Useful for watching outbreaks that could affect travel, supply chains, humanitarian stress, or regional operating conditions.
items.notMeaning: Not a pandemic declaration and not automatically high severity.
items.example: A WHO alert in a port-heavy region matters more if it overlaps shipping, border controls, or local instability signals.

items.meaning: The change summary between the current sweep and the previous one, including new, escalated, and de-escalated signals.
items.matters: Useful for spotting what changed recently instead of re-reading the full dashboard from scratch.
items.notMeaning: Not a full risk model. It is a directional change layer on top of the raw signals.
items.example: A delta marked risk-off with several new and escalated items means the latest sweep materially worsened the signal mix.
